_meta.note: All copy in this file is sourced from verified codebase features only. Do not add claims not backed by website-feature-inventory.md. Plaintext credential storage is described accurately — do not rephrase as 'encrypted' or 'secure'.

hero.tagline: A minimal SSH and SFTP client for Linux
hero.subheading: Manage SSH connections and remote files without cloud accounts, telemetry, or complexity.

product_description.short: MurmurSSH is a lightweight desktop client for SSH and SFTP, built for Linux. It stores connection profiles locally, supports SSH key, agent, and password authentication, and includes an SFTP file browser with remote file editing.
product_description.medium: MurmurSSH is an open-source desktop application for Linux that lets you manage SSH connections and browse remote files over SFTP. Profiles are saved as plain JSON files on your machine. All credentials stay local — no cloud sync, no accounts, no telemetry. Built with Tauri 2 and Rust.
product_description.tech_stack: Tauri 2 · Rust · Vanilla TypeScript · ssh2 crate · inotify (Linux)

feature_list.title: Profile-based connection management
feature_list.description: Save connection details — host, port, username, auth method, default path — as named profiles. Profiles are stored as plain JSON files you can copy between machines.

feature_list.title: SSH key authentication
feature_list.description: Point to any private key file on disk. Passphrase-protected keys are supported — the passphrase is prompted at connection time and never saved.

feature_list.title: SSH agent authentication
feature_list.description: Delegate authentication to a running ssh-agent. MurmurSSH tries each available identity until one succeeds.

feature_list.title: Password authentication
feature_list.description: Enter a password at connection time. Choose whether to save it: not at all, on this machine only (plaintext file with 0600 permissions), or inside the profile file (plaintext, portable).

feature_list.title: Host key verification
feature_list.description: Unknown host keys are shown with their SHA-256 fingerprint before you trust them. You can accept once (session only) or save the key to disk. If a stored fingerprint no longer matches the server, the connection is blocked.

feature_list.title: SFTP file browser
feature_list.description: Browse remote directories with breadcrumb navigation and a clickable path input. Upload, download, delete, rename (backend only), and create files and folders.

feature_list.title: Recursive folder operations
feature_list.description: Download an entire remote folder to a local directory. Delete a folder and all its contents (confirmation required).

feature_list.title: Remote file editing
feature_list.description: Open a remote text file in your local editor. When you save, MurmurSSH uploads the changes back — automatically or after confirmation, depending on your setting. Files larger than 1 MB or binary files are rejected.

feature_list.title: SSH terminal sessions
feature_list.description: Launch an SSH session in your system terminal (x-terminal-emulator) with one click. Password auth reuses the SFTP session credential via ControlMaster — no re-prompt needed.

feature_list.title: Optional password saving
feature_list.description: Choose per-connection: don't save (default), save to a machine-local file with 0600 permissions, or save inside the profile JSON. SSH key passphrases are never saved under any setting.

feature_list.title: Light and dark themes
feature_list.description: Catppuccin Latte (light) and Catppuccin Mocha (dark) color palettes. Follows your OS preference by default, switchable in settings.

feature_list.title: Fully local storage
feature_list.description: All profiles, settings, and credentials are stored in ~/.config/murmurssh/ on your machine. Nothing leaves your computer.

feature_list.title: SSH key compatibility for mounted filesystems
feature_list.description: If a private key has permissions that OpenSSH would reject (e.g., on a mounted filesystem), MurmurSSH can create a 0600 local copy for the terminal session. The original key is never modified. The copy is deleted when you disconnect.

feature_list.title: Free and open source
feature_list.description: MIT license. No paid tier, no feature gates. Source available on GitHub.

supported_workflows.title: Connect and browse remote files
steps: Create a profile with host, username, and auth method
Click Connect
Accept or verify the host key on first connect
Enter password or passphrase if prompted
Browse remote directories in the SFTP file browser

supported_workflows.title: Edit a remote file locally
steps: Connect to a profile
Navigate to a remote text file (≤ 1 MB, non-binary)
Click Edit
File opens in your local editor
Save in your editor — MurmurSSH uploads the change automatically or asks for confirmation

supported_workflows.title: Transfer files
steps: Connect to a profile
To upload: click Upload, select a local file (file picker opens in local_path if configured)
To download: select a remote file, click Download (saves to local_path directly, or opens a save dialog)
To download a folder: select a remote folder, click Download — the entire directory tree is copied locally

supported_workflows.title: Open an SSH terminal session
steps: Connect to a profile via SFTP
Click Terminal in the toolbar
An SSH session opens in your system terminal (x-terminal-emulator)
For password auth: terminal reuses the existing session — no re-prompt
For key auth: terminal uses the key file directly; passphrase prompted interactively by the terminal

supported_workflows.title: Move a profile to another machine
steps: Locate your profile JSON in ~/.config/murmurssh/profiles/
Copy the file to the same path on another machine
Open MurmurSSH — the profile appears in the selector
Note: SSH key paths are absolute and may need updating on the target machine

screenshot_captions.note: No screenshots are present in the repository as of audit date. Captions below describe what each screen would show based on the implemented UI. Update this section with actual image filenames once screenshots are taken.

screens.caption: The SFTP file browser showing a remote directory listing. Toolbar buttons (Disconnect, Terminal, Home, Up, Refresh) at top. Breadcrumb navigation below. File table with name and size columns. Action buttons (Upload, Download, Edit, Delete, ＋ File, ＋ Folder) at the bottom.

The profile creation form. Fields for display name, host, port, username, authentication type (key/agent/password), key file path (with Browse button), default remote path, local path, editor command, and upload mode.
The host key verification dialog. Shows the server's SHA-256 fingerprint with three options: Cancel, Accept once, Accept and save.
The password prompt with three save-mode options: Don't save, Save on this PC only, Save in profile file. A warning is shown when the portable option is selected.
The settings dialog with profile storage path configuration and theme selector (System / Dark / Light).

release_and_install.status_note: This is a beta release. Core features are functional but bugs are expected. Please report issues via the GitHub issue tracker.
install_steps: Download the latest .deb file from the GitHub Releases page
Launch MurmurSSH from your application menu or run murmurssh from a terminal
runtime_requirements: Ubuntu 22.04 or later, or compatible Debian-based Linux

commands: npm run tauri dev       # development with hot reload
npm run tauri icon path/to/icon.png  # generate icons (required before release build)
npm run tauri build     # produces .deb in src-tauri/target/release/bundle/deb/

known_limitations: Only one profile can be active at a time
Deleted files and folders cannot be recovered — there is no trash or undo
Remote file editing is limited to text files under 1 MB
Each SFTP operation opens a fresh connection — not optimized for rapid sequential file operations
Linux only — no Windows or macOS support
File rename has no UI button (backend support exists; no frontend control)
Workspace cache (~/.config/murmurssh/workspace/) is not cleaned up automatically
what_murmurssh_does_not_do: No cloud sync or remote settings storage
No user account or login required
No telemetry or usage data collection
No built-in terminal emulator — uses your system terminal
No integrated text editor — uses your system editor
No port forwarding or SSH tunnels
